message:
docs(extensions): regenerate de-biased onboarding queue + beads sync

- docs/extension-onboarding-queue.json: regenerated from the
  1000-candidate pool with de-biased ranking that penalizes
  shared-repo popularity inflation and focuses Pi-relevant extensions
  (bd-3sxyz). Net change: ~20K lines reordered/rescored as the
  ranking algorithm now weights Pi-specific utility higher than raw
  GitHub star count.
- docs/extension-onboarding-queue.md: updated human-readable queue
  reflecting the new ranking order (614 lines changed)
- .beads/issues.jsonl: sync tracker state — updated status on
  bd-1f42.1.2.1 (coverage baseline), bd-3sf (extension manifest spec),
  bd-3sgr (termux docs), bd-3so (logging spec), bd-3sxyz (de-bias
  onboarding queue, now in_progress)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-2oz69","title":"[PERF-TEST-E2E] End-to-end performance test scripts","description":"## Scope

E2E test scripts that exercise the full performance pipeline with realistic workloads and structured logging.

### Script 1: Long Conversation Responsiveness
```
Setup: Create 500-message synthetic conversation via VCR
Action: Measure frame times during scroll-to-top, scroll-to-bottom
Assert: p95 frame time < 16ms (60fps budget)
Logging: JSONL with fields: test_name, frame_index, frame_time_us, cache_hit_ratio, memory_rss_bytes
```

### Script 2: Streaming With History
```
Setup: 200 messages in history, then start streaming
Action: Stream 50 tokens via VCR, measure frame times
Assert: frame time O(token_length), not O(total_conversation)
Logging: JSONL with fields: test_name, token_index, frame_time_us, prefix_valid, streaming_buffer_len
```

### Script 3: Degradation Under Load
```
Setup: Start TUI, inject synthetic slow frames via test hook
Action: Feed 5 consecutive >20ms frames, then 15 fast frames
Assert: Degraded mode triggered, then recovery to Normal
Logging: JSONL with fields: test_name, frame_index, fidelity_level, frame_time_us, consecutive_over, consecutive_under
```

### Script 4: Memory Pressure Response
```
Setup: Start TUI with mocked RSS monitor
Action: Set RSS to 150MB, verify auto-collapse; set to 250MB, verify truncation
Assert: Tool outputs collapsed, old messages truncated, session file unchanged
Logging: JSONL with fields: test_name, memory_level, rss_bytes, messages_visible, messages_truncated, tool_outputs_collapsed
```

## Test Infrastructure
- All tests use common::harness::TestHarness for JSONL logging
- Artifacts written to tests/artifacts/perf/ for CI retention
- Each test emits a summary line: PASS/FAIL with key metrics

## Dependencies
- Depends on PERF-TEST-1 through PERF-TEST-4 (integration tests should pass before E2E)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 4 E2E scripts with JSONL structured logging (pi.test.perf_event.v1 schema)
- [ ] Artifacts written to tests/artifacts/perf/
- [ ] p95 frame time assertions for 500-message conversation
- [ ] Streaming performance isolation verified (O(token) not O(conversation))
- [ ] Degradation + recovery cycle verified
- [ ] Memory pressure response verified (collapse + truncate)
- [ ] All tests pass

- Discover Pi extensions that are distributed primarily via npm packages (often missed by repo-only search).

Search strategy
- Use npm search + registry API to query for:
  - buildwithpi
  - pi-mono
  - pi-coding-agent
  - "Pi Agent" extension
  - extension + @mariozechner/pi-coding-agent
- Also look for extension “bundles” that ship many extensions in one package.

What to capture per candidate
- package name, version, dist tarball URL, repository URL
- weekly/monthly downloads (popularity)
- keywords/tags that indicate it is a Pi extension
- entrypoint file(s) and whether it contains ExtensionAPI import / export default

Output
- A list of npm-based candidates that can be fed into the inventory and then pinned/archived.
- For each package, include a reproducible reference (version + integrity/checksum).

Acceptance criteria
- >= 5 distinct npm queries executed and recorded.
- >= 50 plausible candidates triaged.
- >= 20 new validated “true Pi extension” entrypoints surfaced via npm.

 {"id":"bd-2p8","title":"Extension event hooks + cancellation semantics","description":"Background:
- EXISTING_PI_STRUCTURE defines event hooks (session_before_switch/fork/compact/etc.) that can cancel actions.
- Extension runtime must enforce ordering + cancellation and emit extension_error when handlers fail.

Scope / Steps:
1) Implement event hook dispatch order and payloads for session lifecycle + tool/turn events.
2) Honor cancellation responses (e.g., session_before_switch/fork/compact) and propagate to UI/status.
3) Add tests that verify cancellation prevents side effects and errors surface correctly.
4) Add conformance fixtures for cancellation edge cases.

Acceptance:
- Event hooks run in the correct order and can veto actions.
- Cancellation is observable in UI/RPC and session state is unchanged.
- Tests + fixtures cover both success and cancel paths.

1. **All tests pass**: `cargo test --all-targets` → 0 failures
2. **Clippy clean**: `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` → 0 errors
3. **Format clean**: `cargo fmt --check` → 0 changes

6. **FEATURE_PARITY.md audit**: Every row is ✅ or ⬜ (no 🔶 or ❌ remaining in scope)

### Evidence Dossier

Generate a JSON report:
```json

    "partial": 0,

    "out_of_scope": M
  }
}
```

- Depends on DOC-1 (FEATURE_PARITY.md must be current)
- Depends on all track completions

 {"id":"bd-3t3ct","title":"[PERF-5] Adaptive FPS under CPU pressure via /proc/loadavg monitoring","description":"## Problem

On an overloaded machine (many agents running, heavy compilation, etc.), the TUI should not waste CPU cycles rendering at 60fps when the user probably cannot even see the updates.

## Solution: CPU Pressure Detection + FPS Adaptation

### Detection
- Sample /proc/loadavg every 2 seconds (Linux) or libc::getloadavg() (macOS/BSD)
- Compare 1-minute load average against available CPU count (from std::thread::available_parallelism())
- Pressure levels:
  - load < 1.0 × CPUs → Normal
  - load 1.0-2.0 × CPUs → Moderate pressure
  - load > 2.0 × CPUs → High pressure
  - load > 4.0 × CPUs → Critical pressure

### Adaptation
- Normal: 60fps (default)
- Moderate: 45fps
- High: 30fps
- Critical: 15fps

### Implementation

```rust
struct CpuPressureMonitor {
    cpu_count: usize,
    last_sample: Instant,
    sample_interval: Duration,  // 2 seconds
    current_load: f64,
    pressure_level: PressureLevel,
}

impl CpuPressureMonitor {
    fn sample(&mut self) -> PressureLevel {
        if self.last_sample.elapsed() < self.sample_interval {
            return self.pressure_level;
        }
        self.last_sample = Instant::now();
        
        #[cfg(target_os = "linux")]
        {
            // Read /proc/loadavg: "0.52 0.64 0.71 1/234 12345"
            // Parse first field (1-minute load average)
            if let Ok(content) = std::fs::read_to_string("/proc/loadavg") {
                if let Some(load_str) = content.split_whitespace().next() {
                    if let Ok(load) = load_str.parse::<f64>() {
                        self.current_load = load;
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        
        #[cfg(any(target_os = "macos", target_os = "freebsd"))]
        {
            // libc::getloadavg() — standard POSIX, available on macOS and BSD
            let mut loadavg = [0.0f64; 1];
            unsafe {
                if libc::getloadavg(loadavg.as_mut_ptr(), 1) == 1 {
                    self.current_load = loadavg[0];
                }
            }
        }
        
        let ratio = self.current_load / self.cpu_count as f64;
        self.pressure_level = match ratio {
            r if r < 1.0 => PressureLevel::Normal,
            r if r < 2.0 => PressureLevel::Moderate,
            r if r < 4.0 => PressureLevel::High,
            _ => PressureLevel::Critical,
        };
        self.pressure_level
    }
}
```

### Integration with Frame Budget (PERF-4)
- CPU pressure provides a FLOOR for the frame budget fidelity level
- If CPU pressure is Critical, fidelity cannot be higher than Degraded
- Frame budget can further reduce fidelity if individual frames exceed budget
- Combined: min(cpu_pressure_level, frame_budget_level)

### Platform Support
- Linux: /proc/loadavg (always available, zero-allocation read)
- macOS/BSD: libc::getloadavg() (POSIX standard, no special crate needed)
- Windows/other: disable (always Normal)
- Use #[cfg(target_os)] for compile-time platform selection

### Why This Can Depend on PERF-3 Instead of PERF-4
- PERF-5 only needs the FrameTimingStats counter (for sample interval gating) and the PressureLevel enum
- It does NOT need the full FrameBudget state machine to be implemented
- The integration point is: FrameBudget::effective_fidelity() = min(budget_fidelity, cpu_pressure_level)
- This min() can be added to PERF-4 as a simple method, and PERF-5 provides the cpu_pressure_level input
- **However**: keeping the PERF-4 dependency is cleaner because PERF-4 defines the RenderFidelity enum and the effective_fidelity() method that PERF-5 feeds into

## Files to Modify
- src/interactive.rs: CpuPressureMonitor struct, integration with FrameBudget

## Dependencies
- Depends on PERF-4 (frame budget — this layer feeds into budget decisions with effective_fidelity())

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CpuPressureMonitor reads /proc/loadavg on Linux
- [ ] CpuPressureMonitor uses libc::getloadavg() on macOS/BSD (not sysctl)
- [ ] Pressure levels correctly mapped from load/cpu_count ratio
- [ ] FPS adapted based on pressure level
- [ ] Integration with frame budget state machine via effective_fidelity()
- [ ] Unit tests for pressure level calculation (injectable load value, not reading /proc in tests)
- [ ] Unit tests for combined pressure + budget decision
- [ ] Graceful no-op on non-Linux/macOS platforms (always Normal)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:14:31.312915658Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T04:26:19.432381266Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T04:26:19.432346571Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":1032,"issue_id":"bd-3t3ct","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Absorbed into PERF-4 (bd-anewk).
